Just a side note on the manual testing. 
- We won't really see the status set to unhealthy on the dashboard since they quickly be killed in the next update loop and retrying `ready()`/ `check_health()` in the new instance. The log did clearly state when an unhealthy proxy is shutting down and restarting and when the retries have happened.
- Tried comment out unhealthy proxy kill and seeing the proxy status changed and stayed in unhealthy correctly. 
- Tried to change the state of check calls in between unhealthy and healthy and seeing the expected behavior and the proxy is able to recover from failure or fail and restart. 

Also just to share some code I used to do this manual test. Basically just have either `ready()` or `check_health()` reading a file and trigger exception when there are content in the file
```
# Add to HTTPProxyActor#ready or HTTPProxyActor#check_health
# and change the content of test_health.txt to have check fail or succeed
with open('/Users/gene/Downloads/test_health.txt', 'r') as f:
    if f.readline():
        raise ValueError('fail when there are text in the file')
```
And use `serve start` and `serve shutdown` between code changes. Can view status changes in the dashboard. Great idea from Ed for how do this manual test!

Just a few notes for future PRs:
- When you add new test files, make sure you activate them in our ray.rllib.BUILD file, otherwise, they won't be included in the CI testing.
- When writing new functions/classes and their docstrings, let's make sure we only define type-hints (incl. return type) in the function's signature and not anymore in the docstring's "Args" and "Returns". I know we have to retro-fit more old code to fit this pattern.
- When changing existing (non-private) function signatures, please make sure the old args are still supported (soft-deprecate them first via the tools in ray.rllib.utils.deprecation). For an example, see `ray.rllib.utils.numpy.convert_to_numpy()`.

Gonna block this - I think that the set of things here is too broad. I don't think there is user utility for a lot of stuff like `can_subset` for example, and casting too wide a net on what properties/methods we directly communicate might make life difficult in the future. It also opens up a can of worms around APIs that aren't part of the public API but might be exposed through excessive property visibility. For example, if `AssetsDefinition.can_subset` is public, should `OpDefinition.compute_fn` be public (which returns a currently private class)? I think drawing that distinction is hard, and fwiw our docs don't have this level of detail right now. Look at JobDefinition's API docs https://docs.dagster.io/_apidocs/jobs#dagster.JobDefinition, which only expose `execute_in_process` and `with_hooks`.

Approving this change although I have a some uncertainties.

* Could you please say something about the "why" in the PR description? Is the `static_assert` in `isinstance` the main motivation? What accidents does that prevent?

* The naming seems to refer back to `class generic_type`. Is that a correct understanding? Would it make sense to add a comment to explain? — Pegging `is_generic_type` on the `type_caster` seems like "circumstantial evidence" (as opposed to direct evidence). Is a direct `is_generic_type` too difficult to implement? My guess: yes, but it would be good to explain (tersely is fine).

* `class generic_type` is in pybind11.h
* A `struct is_generic_type` forward declaration is in pytypes.h
* The `is_generic_type` specializations are in cast.h

This makes me wonder: is the `isinstance` overload for `detail::isinstance_generic` in the right place? Could it be moved to cast.h?

I'm OK if the answer is no, the current pybind11 code organization has me wishing a lot and fixing that is obviously a bigger project. But if moving the `isinstance` overload is an option, that might help at least a little bit.

First, now that this function can potentially return None, some of the type signatures downstream of this are inaccurate (start_sync, get_job_status, get_connection_details), because they say they'll return a dictionary (which they always should).

To fix the type errors, you can do

`from dagster import check`

at the top, and then wrap those return values in `check.is_dict(...)`. This will ensure that if these functions get a None return value, there will be an error.

Once those type issues are cleaned up, you should run `make black` at the top level of the repo to make sure everything is formatted properly.

It'd also be great if you could add a quick test to verify this new behavior.

This PR looks great!  I didn't know (or maybe forgot) that jobs could create annotations like this.  Very cool.  Seems to work as intended.

One thing to note is that this job flags all errors in the changed files, whereas I think (although my memory is a bit hazy) our former setup with stickler-ci wouldn't flag unchanged lines.  The old behavior had pros and cons: it wouldn't pester the contributor with errors unrelated to their PR, but it also wouldn't necessarily catch things like unused imports.  In our case, flagging all errors means making a tiny change to some files will result in dozens of errors.  See for example: https://github.com/pvlib/pvlib-python/actions/runs/5476853711/jobs/9975004553?pr=1786

Is there some way to not report those unrelated errors?  Unless we go through and fix all of those (which I'm not sure is a good idea), I think they will make this CI job a lot less useful for many PRs.

Something else is that there is a maximum of 10 annotations.  Not really a big deal.  You can always look at the job text output to see the full list of lint errors.

> For reference, these are currently excluded in this PR

I think @reepoi just copied those exclusions over from our current lint configuration in `.stickler.yml`.  We can certainly talk about changing those rules, but let's focus on just getting the linter running again in this PR.

**Design question:** At the API level, could we consolidate `prefetch_blocks` and `async_fetch_batch` with a single `prefetch_batches` argument that gives the number of batches to asynchronously prefetch?

## Motivation

I think this will be a lot cleaner from an end-user perspective, with a single argument controlling prefetching behavior that has clear batch-based semantics. We ultimately care about **pipelining upstream batch preparation with downstream batch computation**, and both prefetching batches over the network and background batch slicing/concatenation/formatting fall under the former, so having a single batch-based argument controlling how many batches we want to prepare while a single batch is being processed seems like better UX. IMO, the object store transfer prefetching should probably be an implementation detail in facilitating a generic "how many batches to we want to prepare in the background on this worker" API, similar to the semantics of the [`tf.data` prefetching API](https://www.tensorflow.org/api_docs/python/tf/data/Dataset#prefetch).

## Do We Need Two Knobs?

Since this seems like nicer UX, the only reasons that we'd want to have two knobs (that I can think of) are:
1. We want to have different levels of prefetching for object store transfers compared to background slicing/concatenation/format conversion.
2. The prefetching unit for the two args should be different (e.g. batch vs. block).

(1) could be true. If we assume that we're not hitting NIC bandwidth or CPU bottlenecks during deserialization or batch prep, then prefetching object store transfers is ultimately limited by the node's object store capacity, while background slicing/concatenation/format conversion is ultimately limited by the node's worker heap, which are separate resource pools with different capacities. This is further complicated by format conversions leading to memory inflation. E.g. for a Pandas batch format UDF on Arrow blocks executed on a node that has equal object store and worker heap allocations, you could imagine that we want to prefetch 8 batches to the object store, but only do 1 background batch slice and format conversion since the Pandas format conversion results in a DataFrame that's 4x larger than the Arrow data (and the UDF will need to at least allocate an output in the worker heap as well); this inflation could easily happen for boolean data, for example, which is bitpacked in Arrow but is not in Pandas.

However, this may be a non-issue if:
* the Python worker heap is typically the bottleneck rather than the node's object store (I think this is true for transformations/trainers that don't use a NumPy/Arrow narrow waist), or;
* we can rely on Ray Core's object pull manager to do proper backpressure on object transfers (I believe this is true).

The user can set `prefetch_batches` tailored to whatever eliminates compute resource stalls subject to a Python worker heap cap, and object transfer prefetching will do a best-effort fulfillment of `prefetch_batches`, subject to Ray Core's backpressure on object transfers when the node's object store pressure gets high. I'm thinking that this might make (1) a non-issue.

(2) is the status quo, but I think that the current object store prefetching API would be better expressed in terms of batches (this has been a long-standing TODO), so I don't think that (2) is a concern.

## Semantics and Implementation

For `.map_batches()`, this `prefetch_batches` arg would have the existing `async_fetch_batch` semantics, except that the buffer size is configurable. Down the line, we might have this arg control the number of blocks that are prefetched by actor workers under the actor compute strategy, rather than that being a strategy config.

For `.iter_batches()`, this `prefetch_batches` arg would have the existing `async_fetch_batch` semantics along with exposing the buffer size, _and_ would control how many blocks are prefetched into the object store on the consumer/worker node. Namely, if we know the number of rows in a block from the readily-available block metadata (this should be defined in the common case), we would prefetch `N` blocks such that `N` is the smallest number of blocks that satisfies `prefetch_batches`, i.e. we choose an `N` such that `sum(b.num_rows for b in blocks[:N]) >= prefetch_batches * batch_size` and `sum(b.num_rows for b in blocks[:N-1]) < prefetch_batches * batch_size`. If the block metadata doesn't have the number of rows in the block (e.g. the dataset is a lazy read of CSV files, for which we don't know the number of rows until the entire files has been read), then we can prefetch one block at a time.

The only open question for me is why we don't have any overloads for `add_attachment` and the init of the attachment or maybe I'm missing something. 

Either you want to pass bytes for an attachment or a file to read. So two methods would make sense for me. For the first method, you are forced to pass `bytes` and a `filename` for the second you only need to pass a `path`. This forces you to use the API right at compile time and you don't find out at runtime if you passed in the wrong arguments. We could ditch raising `TypeError` in the init of the `Attachment`. What do you think @mitsuhiko about that?

```
def add_attachment(
    self,
    bytes=None,  # type: bytes
    filename=None,  # type: str
    content_type=None,  # type: Optional[str]
    add_to_transactions=False,  # type: bool
)

def add_attachment(
    self,
    path=None,  # type: str
    filename=None,  # type: Optional[str]
    content_type=None,  # type: Optional[str]
    add_to_transactions=False,  # type: bool
)
```

Other comments are just nitpicking.

>     while True:

This means that if the server is disabled via `vcd cse system disable` the watchdog thread will exit.

I think the logic should be to exit only if the service is in "STOPPED" state.
If the service is in "RUNNING" state and the consumer dies , then restart it.
If the service is in "DISABLED/STOPPING" state then don't restart the consumer but keep the watchdog thread alive.

These filters should be unit tested (there are tests for the test name filter).

Additionally, I don't think we should introduce more filter fields, but rather should extend our existing filter field to more options. I've raised this offline, but my proposal would be something like this:

```
filters = filter_str.split(" ")
for filter in filters:
    if filter.startswith("name:"):
        value = filter[5:]
        apply_filters = NameFilter(value)
    elif filter.startswith("team:"):
        value = filter[5:]
        apply_filters = TeamFilter(value)
    # ...
    else:
        # Default (no prefix): name regex
        apply_filters = NameRegexFilter(filter)  # or `NameFilter(value, regex=True)`
```

For each test we can then loop through the apply_filters and only proceed if all match. Regular filters would be `in` filters, i.e. `name:xgboost` should match `xgboost_train_small` and also `tune_xgboost_sweep`. We don't need regex support for groups and teams (or we could introduce something like `team+:team_regex` or so).
Name regex filter should work like right now as a regex match (not search). This is useful to keep backwards compatibility but also to allow e.g. specifying several tests, e.g. via `long_running_many_drivers|tune_network_throughput` which currently works.

I'm a strong :-1: on this for the QtPy 2.0 release, because:

* We've tested and maintained compatibility all the way up to the release, so it doesn't really make sense to suddenly drop it now and make it impossible for Python 3.6 users to install, despite it being fully functional
* There isn't a compelling benefit to do so immediately; no compat code was simplified/removed and it didn't simplify the CI setup at all (at least as of the current state of the PR); running `pyupgrade --py37-plus` over the full codebase, only one line was changed, and that one only trivially.
* It means all of our tens of thousands of downstream dependencies will either have to drop Python 3.6 support or not upgrade to QtPy 2.0 until they do, and there will be never be a supported combination of Qt6 and Python 3.6
* All of our supported bindings (Pyside2, Pyside6, PyQt5, PyQt6) all still support Python 3.6, so it would be premature to drop it before any of them do
* Checking the [PyPI download stats](https://pypistats.org/packages/qtpy), besides the large spike for 3.7 on Linux (likely driven by CI/automation systems), over the past six months Python 3.6 was a strong number 3 behind Python 3.8 in popularity for QtPy downloads, occasionally even exceeding it.

Furthermore, if we do decide to merge this, there are several specific additional items that need to be investigated/confirmed, and this blocks work on #269 until it is closed or resolved since it touches the same lines.

I strongly recommend that we close this PR for now, retain the existing Python 3.6 support in QtPy 2.0.0, and then drop it in a future 2.1.0 or a later minor version after the EoL, subject to revision if PyQt/PySide dropping support or a non-trivial Py36-only bug crops up that I'm not able to easily solve. I'm happy to commit to taking care of this myself, including verifying all the skips, at any point in QtPy 2.1.0 or later and after the EoL next month.

I've played with the example and looked through most of the code and I'm super impressed! Great job Lisa! I wouldn't change very much, just the default behaviour for old users and introduce a very conservative suggestion for the ``global_constraints``.

## Naming schemes

I agree with your naming: we need ``investment_periods`` to distinguish from ``snapshot_periods`` that run through the year. Old PyPSA "overnight" behaviour is often called "multi-period" optimisation, but we need to make clear that we also do multiple investment periods here.

## Default behaviour

The guiding principle is that old users shouldn't have to make any big changes, and that there should be no surprises.

I suggest that n.snapshots stays the same, and can be either MultiIndex or Index.

All PyPSA functions should by default treat MultiIndex snapshots just the same as Index snapshots. This is the case for old PyPSA ``n.lopf(pyomo=False)``, but ``n.lopf(pyomo=True)``, ``n.lpf()`` and ``n.pf()`` all break when using MultiIndex - we should fix this in future releases (but not now).

The multi-period optimisation is the only one to treat MultiIndex differently, and must be activated with:

``n.lopf(pyomo=False, multi_investment_periods=True)``

When this is activated, it checks that ``n.snapshots`` is MultiIndex, that level 0 is integer, and that the integers are increasing. It also checks that all investment periods appear in the index of ``n.investment_period_weightings`` and vice-versa, throwing an error if not.

If ``n.lopf(pyomo=True, multi_investment_periods=True)`` is called, an error is thrown.

### Snapshot weightings

This is a parallel change to the investment periods. In PyPSA Version 0.18.0 we will replace the pd.Series ``n.snapshot_weightings`` with a pd.DataFrame with three columns ``[objective_weightings, generator_weightings, store_weightings]``. This separates out the three current use cases of weightings in the LOPF:

- ``objective_weightings``: for operational costs in the objective function
- ``store_weightings``: for elapsed time in the filling of ``Store`` and ``StorageUnit`` components
- ``generator_weightings``: for the contribution of generators to ``GlobalConstraint`` components for e.g. CO2 budgets

Example use cases:

- case 1 3-hourly sampling for full year: ``objective_weighting = generator_weighting = store_weighting = 3``.
- case hourly sampling for 10 days to represent full year with cyclic storage: ``objective weighting = generator_weighting = 52. , 
store_weighting = 1.``

BUT this case fails if e.g. a ``Store`` represents non-cyclic depletion of e.g. biomass or some other finite resource, in which case you want ``store_weighting = 52``, like ``generator_weighting``. This interface need all storage doing short-term cycling, or all storage non-cyclic (depleting a finite resource). We should fix this when we implement Kotzur's seasonal storage scheme.

### Investment period weightings

I think the way Lisa implemented this looks good, maybe change the names of weightings to be similar to the ``snapshot_weightings``?

I also suggest not to have ``n.investment_periods``, but track these only via level 0 of ``n.snapshots``.

### Behaviour when an empty network is generated

If you call ``n = pypsa.Network()`` by default will be created:

- ``n.snapshots = pd.Index(["now"])``
- ``n.snapshot_weightings = pd.DataFrame(1.,1.,1.,index=["now"],
                                     columns=["objective_weightings","store_weightings","generator_weightings"])``
- ``n.investment_period_weightings = pd.DataFrame(columns=["objective_weightings","store_weightings","generator_weightings"])``

### Behaviour when a network from PyPSA Version < 0.18.0 is imported

Create new empty ``n.investment_period_weightings``.

The values of pd.Series ``n.snapshot_weightings`` are copied across all three values of the new pd.DataFrame ``n.snapshot_weightings``.

## Global constraints for ``p_nom_max`` at each bus, carrier and investment_period

I suggest to keep these all in ``n.global_constraints`` since they are global in the sense of affecting multiple components at once. We need to add to the ``GlobalConstraint`` table attributes ``bus``, ``carrier`` and ``investment_period`` and type ``multi_investment_period_p_nom_max`` or similar.

Yes, this will create lots of global constraints, but introducing e.g. ``n.global_constraints_p`` doesn't help since you need 3-d information.

This way is minimally invasive.

## Treatment of sub_networks

I think the current way is good. The sub_networks are generated as needed, and the last one to be generated is stored. There is no need to store them from all investment periods, i.e. no need for ``n.sub_networks_p``.

The sub_network is anyway more of a hidden variable.

## Storage behaviour between investment periods

Did I understand correctly that cyclic storage is cyclic within each investment period? This behaviour would be fine.

If the storage is not cyclic, is energy transfered between investment periods? Probably this is not such a good idea for e.g. depletable biomass. But maybe we should model depletable biomass as a generator, rather than as a store that starts full. Then the generator can fill the storage at the start of each year?

## Check objective function

Since the objective function is quite complicated now, please check for some non-trivial examples that the Gurobi objective function is also what you calculated from the results. This should help to find bugs.

## Coding style

Try to avoid abbreviations like ``inv_p`` and ``sns`` in API functions like ``network.determine_network_topology``. Here we should be explicit for understandability. You can abbreviate inside functions, that's fine.

Also try to avoid committing code very specific to your own setup, like paths to libraries.

I added some inline comments on the current PR. 

Other minor comments/ideas related to AreaDefinition attributes and properties:
- For AreaDefinition, many methods are not clear if they return data in xy or lat/lon; the pixel centroid or the outermost pixel corner. Maybe would be worth adding a common suffix (i.e. `_ll`) when they return lat/lons, and otherwise expect they return xy coords. This will enhance code readability. 
Example: 
   - `area_def.corners` currently returns the centroid lat/lon coords in the very old spherical object
   - `area_def.outer_boundary_corners` currently returns the corners lat/lon coords in the very old spherical object
   --> Possible new methods: `corners_centroids`, `corners`, `corners_centroids_ll`, `corners_ll`
- Add utility properties like `upper_left_corner_ll`, `upper_left_centroid_ll`, 
  A possible pattern: `<lower/upper>_<right/left>_<''/'ll'>`  (or bottom instead of lower).
  Currently the `upper_left_corner `is provided by `upper_left_extent  `
- Add `xy_bbox `and `ll_bbox ` properties for consistency with satpy? Equivalent to `area_extent `and `area_extent_ll`
- Current `area_extent_<>` and `<>_bbox` follow the `(xmin, ymin, xmax, ymax)` convention. More precisely the `area_extent` is defined as `(lower_left_x, lower_left_y, upper_right_x, upper_right_y)`. 
   - This is also the convention adopted by rasterio/rioxarray/shapely object `bounds` method.
      --> Maybe we could add a `bounds` property?
   - When retrieving the `area_extent_ll` (in lat/lon) the lower-left and upper-right corners do not necessarily currently define the outermost lat/lon coordinates of the area. Maybe this method should be changed! And maybe we could also add a `bounds_ll` method.
   - Additionally, the area_extent convention differs from the i.e. matplotlib cartopy and matplotlib `extent` which is defined as `(xmin, xmax, ymin, ymax)`
      --> Maybe we could add a `<mpl/cartopy>_area_extent_<''/ll>` property?
- For GEO AreaDef, all the above _ll methods currently return `np.nan`/`np.inf` values. Maybe ad-hoc processing using `get_geostationary_bounding_box_in_lonlats` could be implemented for `area_extent_ll`, but the corners would remain undefined ! Maybe would be worth raising an error to avoid downstream bugs !

It looks good to me!

I see that you've unified the `read_pagedesc` function by introducing an `isbit` flag, to divide by 1.0 in the usual case, which is okay. I personally would have just had two functions. (It was [Lua's documentation](https://www.lua.org/pil/2.3.html) that convinced me to ignore my instinctive fear of using floating point numbers for integer purposes as long as the values remain in the set of integers or fractions of 2 of integers. And as long as performance is not an issue, which is true here.)

Also on style, you have quite a few one-letter variables, many of them capital letters. There's a style guide (PEP 8?) that says that variables should start with a lower-case, and a one-letter capital technically violates that, though you tend to use them for types and template parameters in compiled languages have traditionally been one-letter capitals.

Just as a question, how well do you control the use of dtypes (i.e. your variables and function arguments named `dtype`)? These can mean a few different things:

   * NumPy `np.dtype` instances, which have the most information (including, for instance, endianness and even units on temporal types) but "`np.dtype`" is only one Python type. The distinctions among integers, floats, booleans, etc. are different _values_ of this type. A NumPy scalar cannot have Python type `np.dtype` (e.g. `isinstance(np.int32(123), np.dtype)` is false).
   * NumPy numeric types, such as `np.int32` and `np.float64`. Each of these is a distinct Python type, and NumPy scalars can have these types (e.g. `isinstance(np.int32(123), np.integer)` is true).
   * Python types, such as `int`, `float`, and `bool`. (`isinstance(np.int32(123), int)` is false.)
   * Strings that represent dtypes, such as `"int32"` or `"i4"` for `np.dtype(np.int32)`, which you don't seem to be using.

The reason I ask is because you're using Python `bool` for the dtype of the boolean column, but this is different from NumPy's boolean type, which is `np.bool_`. They even have different numeric towers: Python's opinion is that booleans are a subtype of integers:

```python
>>> issubclass(bool, int)
True
```

but NumPy thinks that booleans are distinct from integers:

```python
>>> issubclass(np.bool_, np.integer)
False
```

(Python is wrong, by the way. Especially about [truthiness](https://stackoverflow.com/q/39983695/1623645), which I consider to be the worst feature of the Python language because it's not just dynamic typing, which is fine, it's actually _weak_ typing, which is not fine. Weak typing made Perl completely unusable, and that's what sent me to Python in the first place.)

The interchangeability of these things favors hacking away at a data analysis, but it can make a mess of programming in the large, so we just need to be conscious of these distinctions and use them consistently. Personally, I use `np.dtype` as types in some contexts (when it's good for types to be Python values) and `np.number` subclasses in others (when it's good for types to be Python types), and generally don't use the Python types like `bool` or the string representations at all.
